methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Series A → next-stage step-up multiplier (2.0x) to $140M total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.skip_reason: insufficient narrow peer set in Quantum Computing (found 0 same-stage, 1 adjacent-stage, 0 similar-tier; need ≥3)
methods.rationale: 7 same-sector peers exist in Quantum Computing, but none of the narrow filters (same-stage / adjacent-stage / similar-valuation-tier) yielded ≥3 datapoints. Falling back to all-sector median would be misleading.

private.name: planqc
private.rationale: Same sector (Quantum Computing), same stage (Series A)

data.narrative:
Quantum Art is a Series A quantum computing company founded by Tal David and Nitzan Akerman that has raised $140M to date. The quantum computing sector includes 3 public comps (IONQ, RGTI, QBTS) and 8 private peers (including IonQ, Quantinuum, Rigetti Computing, D-Wave Quantum, and Infleqtion), though the narrow peer set at identical stage and valuation tier remains limited.

The stage-progression method is the primary applicable approach here. Using total capital raised ($140M) as a valuation proxy and applying a 2.0x step-up multiplier typical of Series A advancement to the next funding round, the analysis implies a valuation of $280M. This method directly reflects market practice for early-stage quantum companies transitioning out of Series A.

The peer-median approach is not applicable given insufficient same-stage, adjacent-stage, and similar-tier peers within the quantum computing universe (fewer than 3 datapoints meeting narrow filters). A sector-wide median would introduce undue heterogeneity across different maturity levels and technology approaches.

The triangulated analysis, anchored primarily on stage-progression, supports a valuation range of $238M–$322M, with a median of $280M. Key uncertainties include the strength of Quantum Art's differentiation within the crowded quantum hardware landscape and the path to meaningful revenue realization at the Series A stage.
